questions.stem: Angle between hour and minute hand at 10:10:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All cups are saucers. Some saucers are plates. No plate is bowl. Conclusions: I. Some cups are plates. II. Some saucers are not bowls.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row facing north: A, B, C, D, E. A is left of B but right of C. E is rightmost. D is left of A. Who is immediate left of E?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 10 m north, 15 m east, 10 m south, 5 m west. Distance and direction from start:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Letter which looks the same in a mirror held to its right:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A points to a photo and says 'He is the son of the only daughter of my father-in-law'. How is the person related to A?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: How many degrees does hour hand move in 25 min?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some red are blue. No blue is green. All green are yellow. Conclusions: I. Some red are not green. II. No blue is yellow.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: P,Q,R,S,T,U sit in 2 rows of 3. Row 1: north, Row 2: south. T is above S. P is below R. U is left of T. Q is above U. Who sits in middle of row 2?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is 3 km north of B. C is 4 km east of A. D is 3 km south of C. B is in which direction of D?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A clock shows 11:10. What is its mirror image time?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: P is Q's brother. R is Q's mother. S is R's father. T is S's mother. How is P related to T?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Today is Friday. What day of week will it be after 61 days?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: No document is confidential. Some confidential are classified. All classified are secret. Conclusions: I. Some documents are classified. II. Some secrets are confidential.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Eight parking spots numbered 1-8. Car P in spot 3. Q is 2 spots right of P. R is left of Q with 1 spot gap. S is adjacent to R. T is not at ends. Which spot is empty?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: From home, school is 6 km west. Market is 8 km south of school. Hospital is 6 km east of market. Hospital from home:

questions.stem: How many straight lines can be drawn through 10 points no three collinear?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the missing: 2, 6, 30, 260, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Should the government provide subsidies for coal washeries? Arg I: Yes, cleaner coal reduces pollution. Arg II: No, washeries should be economically viable.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: AZ, BY, CX, DW, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A company's revenue grows 10% each year. If revenue in 2024 was ₹100 cr, the 2026 revenue is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: 1, 4, 27, 256, ?

questions.stem: Find the wrong number: 3, 6, 15, 45, 157.5, 630

questions.stem: Three rows of 3 in a classroom. A sits at center. B sits behind A. C sits left of B. D sits front-left of A. E sits right of C. Who is in front right corner?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the wrong number: 10, 14, 28, 32, 64, 68, 136

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be under the weather':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Renewable energy sources are gaining importance. Q: Solar and wind power are the most promising. R: Their cost has decreased dramatically. S: Many countries have set ambitious renewable energy targets.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To beat around the bush':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Inflation reduces the purchasing power of money. Q: Central banks adjust interest rates to control inflation. R: High inflation can erode savings and reduce living standards. S: Moderate inflation is considered healthy for economic growth.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'Bite the bullet':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: The internet has revolutionised communication. Q: People can now connect instantly across the globe. R: Social media platforms enable sharing of information. S: However, digital divide remains a concern.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To see eye to eye':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: It is the second largest producer of coal in the world. Q: India has vast mineral resources. R: It also has significant reserves of iron ore and bauxite. S: The mining sector contributes substantially to the economy.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To throw in the towel':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Urbanisation is increasing rapidly worldwide. Q: People migrate to cities in search of better opportunities. R: This puts pressure on housing and infrastructure. S: Sustainable urban planning is needed to manage growth.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be up in arms':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Democracy is a system of government by the people. Q: Citizens elect their representatives through free and fair elections. R: These representatives make laws on behalf of the people. S: An independent judiciary ensures that laws are followed.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be on cloud nine':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates shift suddenly. Q: The point of origin is called the epicentre. R: Seismic waves travel through the Earth's crust. S: The Richter scale measures the magnitude of earthquakes.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To burn one's bridges':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Climate change is one of the biggest challenges today. Q: Rising temperatures cause extreme weather events. R: Governments worldwide are taking mitigation measures. S: However, collective action at individual level is also needed.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'A piece of cake':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: He has been suffering ____ fever for a week.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: workers / demanding / are / wages / better / for
options: Workers are demanding for better wages
For better wages workers are demanding
Demanding are workers better for wages
Better wages for workers are demanding
questions.solution: Workers are demanding for better wages

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The situation is ____ by the delay in approvals.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To face the music':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ARDENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To rest on one's laurels':

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of DILIGENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PROCRASTINATE:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Write allocate (fetch-on-write):
options: Writes directly to memory on miss
Loads the cache line on a write miss before writing
questions.solution: Write-allocate: good for write-back (subsequent writes hit). No-write-allocate: for write-through.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An undecidable problem has:
options: No algorithm that always halts with the correct answer
questions.solution: Undecidable = no Turing machine can solve it for all inputs.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A variable declared inside a function has:
options: Local scope (visible only within the function)
questions.solution: Local variable = block scope. Global = visible everywhere.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 500 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The cache line size is typically:
questions.solution: Typical cache line = 64 bytes (the unit of data transfer between cache and memory).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} is:
questions.solution: a^n b^n requires counting → CFG (S → aSb | ε). Not regular (pumping lemma).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: C++ move semantics:
options: Transfers resources from a temporary object to another object (avoids expensive copies)
questions.solution: Move: T(T&& other) { this->ptr = other.ptr; other.ptr = nullptr; }. std::move casts to rvalue reference.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Multilevel queue scheduling:
options: Processes are assigned to different queues based on type
questions.solution: Multilevel queue: foreground (RR) + background (FCFS) queues with priority scheduling.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A deterministic PDA (DPDA) recognizes:
options: A proper subset of CFLs (deterministic CFLs)
questions.solution: DPDA < PDA (NPDA). DPDA recognizes DCFLs (e.g., L = {a^n b^n}, but not palindromes).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A macro in C is expanded by:
questions.solution: Macro (#define): preprocessor replaces text before compilation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly occurs in which page replacement algorithm?
questions.solution: Belady's: FIFO can show increasing page faults with more frames.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block can be placed in:
questions.solution: Direct mapping fixes each block to one line (block mod #lines), unlike associative caches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Curry-Howard correspondence:
options: Types correspond to logical propositions; programs correspond to proofs
questions.solution: Curry-Howard: implication = function type, conjunction = product type, disjunction = sum type, ∀ = Π, ∃ = Σ.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A buffer overflow occurs when:
options: A file is opened too many times
Data written exceeds the allocated buffer size
questions.solution: Buffer overflow: writing past array bounds (security vulnerability).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The scheduler selects:
options: Which process/thread runs next on the CPU
questions.solution: Scheduler: makes scheduling decisions (short-term scheduler = dispatcher).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The clock speed of a CPU is measured in:
questions.solution: Clock speed = cycles per second (Hz). GHz = 10⁹ Hz.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A DFA for a language L accepts a string if:
options: The string leads to a final state
questions.solution: DFA acceptance = start state → final state after reading entire input.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: C++20 Modules:
options: Replace header files with a more efficient compilation model (import/export)
questions.solution: Modules: export module foo; import <iostream>;. Faster compilation, better encapsulation than headers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock requires NO preemption; allowing preemption actually prevents deadlock.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPU fetches instructions from:
questions.solution: Fetch: PC → address → memory → instruction → IR.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Chomsky normal form (CNF) restricts productions to:
options: A → BC or A → a (where B, C are non-terminals)
questions.solution: CNF: A → BC (two non-terminals) or A → a (terminal).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Object-oriented programming features include:
questions.solution: OOP: encapsulation (data hiding), inheritance (reuse), polymorphism (many forms), abstraction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly — more frames causing more page faults — can occur with which replacement policy?
questions.solution: FIFO can exhibit Belady's anomaly; stack algorithms like LRU and OPT cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The write-through cache policy:
options: Writes data to both cache and main memory simultaneously
questions.solution: Write-through: cache + memory updated together. Write-back: memory updated on eviction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Church-Turing thesis:
options: Everything computable by an algorithm is computable by a Turing machine
questions.solution: Church-Turing: any effectively computable function is TM-computable. Extended: quantum computers ≠ more powerful.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thread-local storage (TLS):
options: Each thread has its own copy of a variable (declared with _Thread_local / thread_local)
questions.solution: TLS: in C11 _Thread_local. In C++11 thread_local. Implemented via TLV (Thread-Local Storage).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Ostrich algorithm ignores:
options: Deadlocks (assume they are rare enough to not worry)
questions.solution: Ostrich: pretend deadlocks never happen (common in practice for rare deadlocks).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Microprogramming is:
options: Implementing the control unit using micro-instructions stored in control memory
questions.solution: Microprogrammed control: ROM stores microcode for each instruction (easier to design).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Simply typed lambda calculus:
options: Adds types to lambda terms (each variable has a type)
questions.solution: Simply typed λ-calculus: all terms halt (strong normalization). No fixed-point combinator (no general recursion).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pass by reference in C++ allows:
options: The function to modify the original argument
Create a copy of the argument
questions.solution: Reference (&): acts as alias to original variable (no copy, modifiable).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing occurs when:
options: A process spends more time paging than executing
questions.solution: Thrashing = high page fault rate, low CPU utilization.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DMA (Direct Memory Access):
options: Allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement
questions.solution: DMA: offloads data transfer from CPU (cycle stealing or burst mode).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The lambda calculus was introduced by:
questions.solution: Lambda calculus: formal system for function definition and application. Basis of functional programming.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The C preprocessor #include directive:
options: Links a library at compile time
Inserts the contents of the specified file at that point
Copies the file to the output
questions.solution: #include: file inclusion (textual include). <> = system headers, "" = user headers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CPU performance equation is:
options: Time = Instruction count × CPI × Cycle time

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Kleene recursion theorem:
options: A program can obtain its own source code / index
questions.solution: Kleene's recursion theorem: for any computable f, there exists e such that φ_e = φ_{f(e)} (self-reference).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The sizeof() operator in C returns the size in:
questions.solution: sizeof returns size in bytes (char = 1, int = 4 typically).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A branch hazard is resolved by:
options: Branch prediction + speculation + branch target buffer
questions.solution: Branch prediction: static (always taken/not-taken) or dynamic (history-based). Mispredict = flush.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Primitive recursive functions:
options: Built from basic functions using composition and primitive recursion (always halt)
questions.solution: Primitive recursive: zero, successor, projection + composition + primitive recursion. Ackermann not primitive recursive.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A segmentation fault occurs when:
options: A program tries to access memory it doesn't have permission to access
questions.solution: Segfault = invalid memory access (null pointer dereference, buffer overflow).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The file permission 755 means:
options: Owner: r-x, Group: r-x, Others: r-x
Owner: rwx, Group: rwx, Others: rwx
Owner: rwx, Group: r-x, Others: r-x

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Turing recognizable languages are also called:
questions.solution: RE = language accepted by a TM (may not halt for strings not in language).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'volatile' keyword in C tells the compiler:
options: The variable may change unexpectedly (don't optimize reads)
questions.solution: Volatile: prevent compiler optimizations (e.g., memory-mapped I/O, signal handlers).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 200 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 8 ms. The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A context-sensitive language is recognized by:
questions.solution: CSL: LBA (TM with tape bounded by input length). Type-1 in Chomsky hierarchy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'const' keyword in C:
questions.solution: const = value cannot be modified after initialization.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Monolithic kernel has:
options: All OS services in kernel space (e.g., Linux, Unix)
questions.solution: Monolithic: large kernel (fast internal calls but harder to maintain).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SIMD extensions include:
questions.solution: SIMD: single instruction operates on multiple data elements (packed in wide registers).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CYK algorithm requires:
questions.solution: CYK: bottom-up parsing using CNF. O(n³) time, O(n²) space for string of length n.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in C++ is achieved by:
questions.solution: Polymorphism: base class pointer → derived class method. Virtual table (vtable) per class.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 50 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 64 bits occupies how many bytes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pushdown automaton (PDA) has:
options: An input tape, a stack, and finite control
questions.solution: PDA = NFA/DFA + stack (LIFO). Power = context-free languages.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The load factor α of a hash table is:
questions.solution: Load factor: α = n/m. Higher α = more collisions. Typically keep α < 0.75.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pair programming involves:
options: Two developers working on the same code at one workstation
questions.solution: Pair programming: driver (writes) + navigator (reviews). Promotes knowledge sharing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The candidate key is:
options: A minimal superkey (minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies rows)
questions.solution: Candidate key = minimal superkey (no proper subset is a superkey). One becomes PK.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm fails with:
questions.solution: Dijkstra: greedy, O(E log V) with heap. Bellman-Ford: O(VE), handles negative weights (detects negative cycles).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 6 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree visits the keys in:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST yields keys in ascending order.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging with 4 KB pages and 32-bit address space requires:
questions.solution: 32-bit: 2^32 / 2^12 = 2^20 pages × 4 bytes = 4 MB. Solved by multi-level page tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=8 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=8: O(8 log 8).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 5G network latency target is:
questions.solution: 5G: ultra-low latency ~1 ms (sub-6 GHz and mmWave). 4G LTE = ~20-30 ms.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RISC stands for:
questions.solution: RISC: simple instructions, fixed length, load-store architecture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A circular queue prevents:
questions.solution: Circular queue reuses empty slots, preventing false overflow.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The disjoint set union (union-find) with path compression and union by rank has:
questions.solution: Union-Find: α(n) inverse Ackermann function = effectively constant.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 50×20 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of heap sort is:
questions.solution: Heapsort = O(n log n) in all cases.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×40 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 3 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: K-way merge in external sorting uses:
options: A heap/priority queue to find the smallest among k runs
questions.solution: K-way merge: O(n log k) using a min-heap of size k.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'grep' command is used for:
questions.solution: grep = global regex print (pattern matching in files).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 11, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLS/SSL handshake involves:
options: Certificate exchange, key exchange, cipher suite negotiation
questions.solution: TLS: ClientHello → ServerHello + Cert + KeyExchange → ClientKeyExchange → ChangeCipherSpec + Finished.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Linear search works on unsorted arrays
Binary search requires a sorted array
Binary search works on an unsorted array
Binary search runs in O(log n)
questions.solution: Binary search only works on sorted data — the claim that it works unsorted is false.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 50 slots holds 30 keys. Its load factor is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Radix sort sorts:
options: Digit by digit (LSD or MSD) using a stable sort per digit
questions.solution: Radix sort: O(d·(n+k)) where d = digits. LSD radix sort typically uses counting sort.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 11, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The hexadecimal addition F + 1 gives:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'final' keyword in Java for a variable means:
questions.solution: final variable = const. final class = cannot be inherited. final method = cannot be overridden.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Handle in LR parsing is:
options: The substring matching the RHS of a production being reduced
questions.solution: Handle = RHS of a production that can be reduced at current step.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ALU can be built using:
questions.solution: ALU: multiple functions selected by control lines (MUX selects output).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bipartite graph check uses:
options: BFS/DFS with 2-coloring (alternating colors; conflict → not bipartite)
questions.solution: A graph is bipartite iff it has no odd-length cycles. 2-coloring via BFS.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: FTP uses which ports for control and data?
questions.solution: FTP: port 21 for commands, port 20 for data transfer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of hexadecimal 2A is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 14, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'ls' command in Unix lists:
questions.solution: ls = list directory. ls -l = long format, ls -a = show hidden files.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The default gateway is:
options: The router that connects a local network to other networks
questions.solution: Default gateway = exit router for traffic not destined to local network.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hamming code provides:
options: Single-bit error correction and double-bit detection
questions.solution: Hamming: SECDED (Single Error Correct, Double Error Detect) with extra parity bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL LIMIT clause:
options: Restricts the number of rows returned
questions.solution: LIMIT (MySQL, PostgreSQL) or TOP (SQL Server) or ROWNUM (Oracle).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The octal representation of decimal 63 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language of palindromes over {a, b} is:
questions.solution: Palindrome = ww^R. CFG: S → aSa | bSb | a | b | ε. Not regular.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 1K memory locations?
